definition: Japan's sovereign debt sustainability experience cannot be generalized to the US or other aging economies due to five structural differences that the US fails entirely: positive NIIP, domestic bond ownership dominance, BoJ majority ownership, persistent current account surplus, and deflationary backdrop.
evidence: Japan NIIP +70% GDP vs US negative ~$20T
JGB >90% domestic ownership vs US ~70% declining
BoJ >50% ownership vs Fed ~20% declining via QT
Japan persistent current account surplus vs US ~3% deficit
Japan deflationary backdrop vs US supply-side inflation
Simultaneous global aging eliminates the capital-flow safety valve Japan enjoyed
open_questions: Which of Japan's structural advantages, if any, could the US substitute and how?
Does Japan's experience under a unipolar US security guarantee make it even less replicable?
